title: Why the return of playfulness signals deep nervous system healing
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The moment I knew something had shifted

I was standing in my kitchen, alone, waiting for water to boil. And then it happened. I started to dance. Not a choreographed thing, not a performance. Just a stupid little wiggle, a shoulder shake, a goofy bounce that made no sense and felt completely right.

I stopped. I looked around like someone might have seen me. And then I realized: I couldn't remember the last time I had done something like that. Months? Years? Decades?

Here's the thing. When you've lived through complex trauma, your nervous system doesn't have room for play. It can't. Play requires safety. It requires the kind of deep, embodied knowing that you're not about to be attacked, shamed, abandoned, or overwhelmed. And if you grew up in an environment where your survival depended on hypervigilance, play was a luxury you couldn't afford.

But then something changed. I noticed it in small ways at first. A joke that made me laugh so hard I snorted. A sudden urge to skip down the sidewalk, which I actually did for three steps before my inner critic started screaming. A moment of pure, unguarded silliness with a friend where I forgot to monitor their face for signs of disapproval.

These moments were not just fun. They were signals. Signs that my nervous system was finally, slowly, beginning to trust that it could come out of hiding. That it didn't have to keep the watchtower lit 24/7. That maybe, just maybe, it was safe enough to let its guard down.

And that's what I want to talk about today. Why the return of playfulness is not just a nice side effect of healing, but one of the most reliable indicators that deep, structural change is happening in your nervous system.

What play actually requires from your body

Let me be really clear about something. Play is not the same as having fun. You can have fun while still being guarded. You can enjoy a movie, a meal, a conversation, while your nervous system stays in a state of low-grade vigilance. That's not play. That's distraction with a smile on its face.

Play is different. Play requires your prefrontal cortex to temporarily abdicate its throne. It requires you to stop predicting, planning, scanning, evaluating. It requires a kind of surrender that your traumatized parts find absolutely terrifying.

Think about what happens when a child plays. They fall down, they get back up. They make up rules, then break them. They laugh until they can't breathe. They are completely in their bodies, completely in the moment, completely unconcerned with how they look or what anyone thinks.

That state of being is only possible when your ventral vagal system is online. That's the part of your nervous system that Stephen Porges identified as the social engagement system. It's the branch of your vagus nerve that allows you to feel safe, connected, and present. When it's active, your face softens, your voice modulates, your eyes light up. You can make eye contact without feeling like you're being examined. You can laugh without checking first to see if it's okay.

But here's what most people don't understand. When you've experienced complex trauma, your ventral vagal system gets damaged. It gets overridden by the sympathetic (fight or flight) and dorsal (freeze, collapse, dissociation) branches. Your nervous system learns that safety is not the default state. It learns that vigilance is the price of survival.

And play? Play becomes impossible. Not because you're broken, not because you've lost your sense of humor. But because your body literally cannot access the state that play requires. It's like trying to run a marathon with a broken leg. You can want to, you can try to, but your body knows better.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body as frozen energy. That energy has to be released before your system can return to its natural, rhythmic, playful state. And that release doesn't happen through thinking. It doesn't happen through talking. It happens through sensation, through movement, through the slow, patient work of letting your body know it's safe now.

So when you start to notice playfulness returning, it's not just a mood shift. It's a neurological event. It means your ventral vagal system is strengthening. It means your social engagement system is coming back online. It means your body is starting to believe, at a cellular level, that the danger has passed.

The shame that keeps play locked away

But here's the hard part. Even when your nervous system starts to heal, even when the capacity for play begins to emerge, there's something that can keep it locked away. Shame.

I remember the first time I felt the urge to be silly after years of trauma therapy. I was with a friend, and I wanted to make a stupid joke. The kind of joke that doesn't land with everyone. The kind of joke that might make me look foolish. And I felt it. That familiar tightening in my chest. That voice that said: Don't. You'll look stupid. They'll judge you. You're too old for this. You're too damaged for this. You don't deserve to be light.

That voice is not just your inner critic. It's a protective part of you that learned, probably very early, that being spontaneous was dangerous. Maybe you were punished for being too loud. Maybe you were shamed for being too silly. Maybe you were told to grow up, to be serious, to stop being a burden. Maybe you learned that the only way to stay safe was to be invisible, and play is anything but invisible.

Janina Fisher, who writes beautifully about structural dissociation, talks about how different parts of us hold different responses to trauma. There's the part that's still a child, longing to play. And there's the part that's the protector, the manager, the one who keeps everything under control. These parts are in conflict. The child part wants to come out. The protector part says absolutely not.

Healing involves letting those parts talk to each other. Letting the protector know that it's okay to relax. That you're not a child anymore. That you have resources now. That you can take care of yourself if something goes wrong.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps explain why so many of us learned that being ourselves wasn't safe, and why we developed these hypervigilant, controlling parts in the first place.

When you start to feel the pull toward play, and you also feel the shame that tries to shut it down, that's not a sign that something is wrong. That's a sign that healing is happening. The child part is starting to trust that it can come out. The protector is still doing its job, but it's starting to listen. The conflict is the process. Stay with me here. The return of playfulness is not a smooth, linear thing. It's a negotiation. It's a series of small rebellions against the old rules. It's a slow, awkward, beautiful reclamation of something you never should have lost.

How to invite play back without forcing it

You cannot force your nervous system to heal. You cannot command yourself to be playful. That's not how this works. The return of playfulness is a byproduct, not a goal. It's something that emerges when the conditions are right, not something you can manufacture through willpower.

But you can create conditions that make play more possible. You can start to notice the small moments when your body feels even a little bit safer. You can pay attention to the times when your shoulders drop, when your breath deepens, when your jaw unclenches. Those are the windows. Those are the moments when play might be possible.

And then you can make tiny, almost invisible experiments. You can hum a song while you wash dishes. You can wiggle your toes under your desk. You can make a silly face at yourself in the mirror when no one is watching. You can let yourself have a single, ridiculous thought without judging it.

These are not big things. They are not going to fix everything. But they are signals to your nervous system. They are small acts of rebellion against the tyranny of seriousness. They are ways of telling your body: I see you. I hear you. I'm going to let you have a little bit of freedom now.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. It helps shift the question from "what's wrong with me" to "what happened to me," which is exactly the kind of reframe that allows your nervous system to start relaxing its grip.

And if you find that shame is a particularly stubborn barrier, a gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. It's not about forcing yourself to be happy. It's about making space for what's already here, including the parts that are scared to come out and play.

Here's the truth. The return of playfulness is not always comfortable. It can feel awkward, embarrassing, even frightening. You might cry the first time you let yourself be genuinely silly. You might feel a wave of grief for all the years you spent in survival mode, for all the play you missed, for all the moments you couldn't access. That's okay. That's part of it. Let those feelings come. They are not obstacles to healing. They are healing itself.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. It offers concrete, gentle practices for building ventral vagal safety, which is the foundation that play requires.

What to look for as play returns

So how do you know if this is happening for you? What does the return of playfulness actually look like in real life?

It might look like laughing at something that isn't that funny, just because it feels good to laugh. It might look like making a pun and not apologizing for it. It might look like dancing in your kitchen, even if you're terrible at it. It might look like letting yourself be curious about something without needing to master it. It might look like starting a project you might fail at, just because it's interesting.

It might look like being able to joke about something that used to be too painful to touch. It might look like teasing a friend without worrying that you've gone too far. It might look like letting someone else be silly with you, and not pulling away when they try.

It might look like nothing dramatic at all. Just a subtle lightening. A softening around the edges. A sense that life doesn't have to be so heavy all the time. A feeling that you can breathe a little easier.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did something shift? Did you feel a small release? A tiny opening? That's your nervous system responding to the possibility of safety. That's the beginning.

I want to be honest with you about something. The return of playfulness doesn't mean you're healed. It doesn't mean the trauma is gone. It doesn't mean you won't still have hard days, hard moments, hard years. What it means is that your nervous system has started to build a new pathway. A pathway to safety. A pathway to connection. A pathway to the kind of embodied aliveness that was always meant to be yours.

And that is worth celebrating. Not with a forced smile or a fake positivity. But with a real, genuine, slightly awkward wiggle in your kitchen. With a stupid joke that makes you snort. With a moment of pure, unguarded silliness that reminds you: you are still here. You are still alive. And you are still capable of joy.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • Anchored (paid link)
  • What Happened to You? (paid link)
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link)
